title: Gamma-support, gamma-coisotropic subsets and application
speaker: Claude Viterbo
abstract: To an element in the completion of the set of Lagrangians for the spectral distance we associate a support.  We show that such a support is γ-coisotropic (a notion we shall define in the talk) and we shall give examples and counterexamples of γ-coisotorpic sets that can be (or cannot be) γ-supports.  Finally, we give some applications of these notions to singular support of sheaves (joint work with S. Guillermou) and dissipative dynamics, allowing us to extend the notion of Birkhoff attractor (joint with V. Humilière).

proof_stages.label: Spectral gamma metric on exact Lagrangians
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up exact Lagrangian and Hamiltonian-isotopy spaces, recalls the equivalent generating-function/Floer/sheaf definitions of the spectral metric gamma, lists its invariance and continuity properties, and observes that these metric spaces are not complete or even Baire.

proof_stages.label: Humiliere gamma-completion
proof_stages.summary: The Humiliere completions of the Lagrangian and Hamiltonian groups are introduced as gamma-Cauchy completions, motivated by naturally occurring C0 graphs, homogenization limits, and singular Hamiltonian flows, with the completed Hamiltonian group still acting isometrically on completed Lagrangians.

proof_stages.label: Gamma-support of a completed Lagrangian
proof_stages.summary: A point is defined to lie in the gamma-support of a completed Lagrangian if every neighborhood supports a compactly supported Hamiltonian perturbation that moves the object by positive gamma distance, and the talk establishes basic properties such as agreement with smooth Lagrangians, Hamiltonian invariance, lower semicontinuity, nonuniqueness phenomena, and nonemptiness/intersection constraints.

proof_stages.label: Gamma-coisotropic subsets
proof_stages.summary: Gamma-coisotropicity is defined by a positive gamma-energy lower bound for Hamiltonian maps supported in a small ball that displace the set from a smaller ball, and the notion is shown to be invariant under gamma-preserving homeomorphisms while matching familiar coisotropic behavior in smooth cases.

proof_stages.label: Main support theorems and Peano Lagrangians
proof_stages.summary: The main structural theorem states that every gamma-support is gamma-coisotropic, while a complementary construction produces completed Lagrangians whose support is the zero section union a cube, showing that supports can be large and that fibers over a fixed support can be nonunique or infinite.

proof_stages.label: Regular Lagrangian uniqueness and center of the completion
proof_stages.summary: For compact homogeneous N, if the gamma-support of a completed Lagrangian in T*N is an exact smooth Lagrangian Hamiltonian-isotopic to the zero section, then the completed Lagrangian is that smooth Lagrangian; this regular uniqueness theorem implies, in the torus cotangent case, triviality of the center of the completed Hamiltonian group.

proof_stages.label: Applications to singular Hamiltonians and sheaf singular support
proof_stages.summary: Gamma-coisotropicity is used to formulate removability criteria for singular Hamiltonians and to extend earlier results for small-dimensional singular sets, and joint work with Guillermou proves that sheaf-theoretic singular supports are gamma-coisotropic, strengthening Kashiwara-Schapira involutivity.

proof_stages.label: Dissipative contractions and Birkhoff attractors
proof_stages.summary: A conformally symplectic dissipative map contracts the gamma metric on the completion, hence has a completed fixed point whose gamma-support is an invariant set; in dimension one this gamma-support is identified with the classical Birkhoff attractor, suggesting a higher-dimensional generalization.

examples.name: Unit interval in the symplectic plane
examples.note: The interval [0,1] in R2 is gamma-coisotropic exactly at interior points, and this endpoint phenomenon is later used to explain why the raw dissipative invariant set must be trimmed to the Birkhoff attractor.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through the unit interval in R2, distinguishing interior points from endpoints by explaining when a small-energy local Hamiltonian can push the set away

passes.reason: The ordinary interval is used to show gamma-coisotropy can fail at boundary points.

examples.name: One-dimensional Birkhoff attractor
examples.note: For a dissipative map of T*S1, the gamma-support of the contraction fixed point recovers the classical Birkhoff attractor, serving as the model case for the proposed higher-dimensional generalization.
examples.engagement_reason: he constructs the one-dimensional Birkhoff attractor from iterates of an annulus under a conformally symplectic map and identifies it with the gamma support of the fixed point
